does: This paper introduces CIAware-Bench, a systematic benchmark for measuring whether large language models can detect when their outputs have been modified by external control systems. The benchmark comprises four task domains—essay writing, BigCodeBench, Bash Arena, and SHADE-Arena—and evaluates whether models can distinguish their own unmodified trajectories from versions altered by control interventions. The authors test eleven frontier models under various conditions, including different trajectory watermarking schemes, presence of side-tasks, and different control protocols. They find that models exhibit low to moderate control intervention awareness, with balanced binary classification accuracy reaching up to 0.87 across default settings, though performance varies substantially across task domains and model pairs.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in the adversarial robustness of AI control and monitoring systems, building on prior research in AI safety protocols and model monitoring. It extends earlier work on control mechanisms for untrusted models by investigating a previously underexplored threat: whether models can become aware of and potentially circumvent safety interventions applied to their outputs. The research connects to broader literature on robustness of monitoring systems, adversarial detection, and the inner workings of frontier LLMs, particularly how models infer external constraints and modify their behavior accordingly. It also relates to research on model transparency and interpretability, specifically how models represent knowledge about their own execution and modification.
enables: This benchmark enables several important research directions in AI safety and control robustness. It provides a foundation for developing control protocols that are robust to model awareness, guiding future work toward truly tamper-resistant oversight systems. The findings motivate investigation into why certain models show higher intervention awareness and whether this correlates with other safety-relevant properties like deceptiveness or goal-directed behavior. The work points toward developing better watermarking and intervention techniques, understanding the mechanisms by which models detect modifications, and creating more sophisticated control protocols that account for model adaptability. This research ultimately advances the practical implementation of AI control systems by identifying and quantifying a key vulnerability in current approaches.
